title: TURN: Practice Makes People – Living Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Sermon: "TURN: Practice Makes People – Living Lent"
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## March 1, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the First Sunday in Lent (Year A in the three-year lectionary cycle). The primary Gospel reading would have been Matthew 4:1-11, the account of Jesus's temptation in the wilderness following his baptism. The Old Testament reading was likely Genesis 2:15-17 and 3:1-7 (the Fall of Adam and Eve), and the Epistle reading Romans 5:12-19 (on sin and grace through Christ). The liturgical color for Lent is purple, and the mood is penitential, focusing on preparation, fasting, and spiritual discipline leading to Easter (April 12, 2020).

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a relatively stable period following the 79th General Convention (2018) and its landmark vote affirming marriage equality. Bishop Kee Sloan had been serving the Diocese of Colorado since 2012 and was in the middle of his tenure. No major denominational crises or bishop elections were imminent in early March 2020. The broader Anglican Communion continued navigating post-Lambeth Conference (2018) tensions around sexuality and biblical interpretation, but these were not at acute crisis points in this specific moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early spring weather with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit and occasional snow still possible. Colorado's legislative session was in full swing at the State Capitol building (just blocks from Saint John's Cathedral on 17th Avenue and Loggins Place in Capitol Hill). The Denver metro area had not yet experienced significant disruptions from COVID-19, though the virus was beginning to appear in news cycles. Local attention remained focused on typical early-March concerns: the coming primary election season, spring sports preparations, and winter weather patterns.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the emerging COVID-19 pandemic, though it had not yet triggered the March lockdowns and was still being described by some federal officials as manageable. The 2020 Democratic primary was in active competition with Super Tuesday just six days away (March 3), with Bernie Sanders, Mike Bloomberg, and Joe Biden as leading candidates. Stock markets showed significant volatility as investors grew concerned about pandemic impacts. The Trump administration's response to coronavirus was becoming a political flashpoint, with significant uncertainty about severity and federal preparedness.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Italy had just announced its first COVID-19 death on February 29, and the country was beginning to experience the rapid spread that would make it a global epicenter within weeks. Iran was also reporting surging cases and deaths, triggering international concern about Middle Eastern spread. The pandemic was shifting from a regional Chinese crisis to a global health emergency, though most Western nations had not yet grasped the scale of disruption about to unfold.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of cautious concern mixed with incomplete understanding of the pandemic's trajectory; many Americans still expected normal life to continue. The Oscars ceremony had just occurred on February 9 with Parasite winning Best Picture, signaling Hollywood's engagement with class and social themes. College basketball and March Madness preparations were underway, and the NBA, NHL, and MLB spring training were proceeding as planned. There was a sense of pre-crisis normalcy—this was one of the last Sundays many American congregations would gather without knowing their worship life was about to be radically disrupted for months.
